text: Rendering visible language onto images is one of the most basic open problems in computer vision and also an essential component in applications such as sentence-based image search. In this work, we treat image annotation as a ranking problem, i.e., ranking and retrieving the correct top-ranked sentences from a large set of possible sentences for a given query image. To this end, we build a new large-scale ranking-oriented dataset, Flickr30k (Flickr30k), which is a subset of the Flickr dataset that contains 30, 793 images each associated with 5 reference captions describing scenes, objects, and activities. To see the competitiveness of rendering visible language to images given the absence of strong supervision on image annotation, we show that it is possible to achieve very high rankings for annotating images given the minimal amount of weak supervision provided for the features used to train the language and vision models. We provide extensive insights into (1) how to leverage multiple reference captions during the training of language and vision models and show that combining syntax and semantics is very beneficial, (2) how to trade-off between expensive human judgment and fast automatic evaluation scores, (cite) and finally conclude that the more robust evaluation metric when assessing ranking-oriented image description generators are the evaluation metrics that assess ranked lists of predictions rather than only their top-ranked element. Consequently, we are able to show that rankings produced by ranking-oriented image description generators can be also evaluated automatically, in an efficient manner, without relying on expensive human judgment.
